title: ABC Module and Abstract Methods

overview.what: The `abc` module provides the `ABC` class and `@abstractmethod` decorator to define Abstract Base Classes. These classes cannot be instantiated directly and force child classes to implement specific methods before they can be instantiated. Think of it like a strict contract or a blueprint: a 'Vehicle' blueprint says you must build an 'engine', but you can't drive the blueprint itself. In Python, if a child class inherits from `ABC` but forgets to override an `@abstractmethod`, Python raises an error at instantiation time, not when the method is called.
overview.why: It prevents developers from accidentally creating incomplete subclasses, enforcing a strict API contract across a codebase and catching bugs early.

why_learning_this: You can enforce strict interfaces across large teams.
It enables robust plugin architectures where every plugin is guaranteed to have the required methods.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to inherit from ABC
common_mistakes.explanation: Decorating a method with `@abstractmethod` does nothing if the class doesn't inherit from `ABC` (or use `ABCMeta`). You can still instantiate it.

common_mistakes.title: Calling the abstract class
common_mistakes.explanation: Trying to instantiate the abstract base class itself will raise a TypeError if it has any abstract methods.

recall_questions.q: What happens if you try to instantiate a class that inherits from `ABC` and contains an `@abstractmethod`?
recall_questions.answer: Python raises a TypeError immediately at instantiation.

recall_questions.q: What is the purpose of the `@abstractmethod` decorator?
recall_questions.answer: It marks a method that must be overridden by any concrete child class before the child class can be instantiated.

recall_questions.q: When does Python check if the abstract methods have been implemented: at class definition, at instantiation, or when the method is called?

practice_tasks.title: Enforce a Contract
practice_tasks.prompt: Currently, `PaymentGateway` is just a regular class, and `StripeGateway` inherits from it but doesn't implement `process_payment`. Modify `PaymentGateway` to inherit from `ABC` and decorate `process_payment` with `@abstractmethod`. Then implement `process_payment` in `StripeGateway` so that it returns a string.

understanding_checks.question: What happens when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: The `Animal` class does not inherit from `ABC`, so the `@abstractmethod` decorator has no enforcing effect. The `Dog` class can be instantiated normally.

understanding_checks.question: What happens when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.answer: A TypeError is raised at `d = Dog()`.
understanding_checks.why: Because `Animal` inherits from `ABC` and `speak` is decorated with `@abstractmethod`, `Dog` must implement `speak` before it can be instantiated.

code_walkthrough.focus: w = LazyWorker() # Fails because LazyWorker didn't implement work()

aha_moment.common_guess: It raises a TypeError because of the abstract method.
aha_moment.why: It prints 'Created successfully!'. The `@abstractmethod` decorator only enforces instantiation rules if the class also inherits from `ABC`. Without `ABC`, the decorator does nothing.

challenge.title: Implement an Exporter Interface
challenge.prompt: Create an abstract base class `DataExporter` with an abstract method `export(self, data)`. Create a subclass `JSONExporter` that implements `export` by returning the data wrapped in a string like `'JSON: [data]'`. Create another subclass `CSVExporter` that forgets to implement `export`. Attempting to instantiate `CSVExporter` should fail with a TypeError.
